content.text: Arrow functions are a compact syntax for writing functions introduced in ES6. They are the default way to write functions in modern JavaScript and React — you will see them in every React component for event handlers, callbacks, and array methods. Beyond their shorter syntax, they have one important behavioral difference from regular functions: they do not have their own
When the arrow function body is a single expression, you can omit the curly braces and the
keyword — the expression is returned implicitly. This is called the
When you implicitly return an object, wrap it in parentheses — otherwise JavaScript parses the curly braces as a function body, not an object:
The most important difference is how
works. Regular functions define their own
based on how they are called. Arrow functions
from the surrounding context where they were defined.
In React with function components (which is essentially all React today),
is not used at all — function components are plain functions, not class instances. So the
distinction matters mainly in older class component patterns.
In React: Where Arrow Functions Appear
Inline arrow functions in JSX are convenient but create a new function reference on every render. For most cases this is fine. In performance-sensitive components passed to
to keep the function reference stable.
